blog.heading: Insights & Updates
blog.description: Engineering deep-dives, product thinking, and company news — straight from the Enwiya team.

posts.title: Building Scalable APIs with Node.js and TypeScript
posts.excerpt: How we structure our Express + TypeScript API projects for long-term maintainability — from folder layout to error handling and OpenAPI docs.
posts.author: Enwiya Engineering

content.text: Scaling a Node.js API from a handful of endpoints to hundreds requires deliberate architectural choices from day one. TypeScript makes this tractable — but only when paired with the right project structure and runtime validation.
Feature-First Folder Layout

We organise every API using a feature-first structure. Each feature (users, orders, payments) gets its own directory containing its router, controller, service, and Zod schema. Related code stays co-located, onboarding is fast, and there is no cross-feature coupling.

src/
  features/
    users/
      users.router.ts
      users.controller.ts
      users.service.ts
      users.schema.ts
  middleware/
  lib/
  app.ts
Type-Safe Request Validation with Zod

Every request body and query parameter is parsed through a Zod schema at the controller boundary before any business logic runs. This eliminates an entire class of runtime errors and gives accurate TypeScript types throughout the request lifecycle — no more casting unknown to anything.
Centralised Error Handling

A single Express error middleware handles every thrown error. We define a lightweight AppError class that carries an HTTP status code. Controllers simply throw — they never construct raw HTTP responses. Consistent error shapes across every endpoint come for free.
Auto-Generated OpenAPI Docs
We use zod-to-openapi to generate an OpenAPI 3.1 spec directly from our Zod schemas. The spec is served at /docs as a live Swagger UI — always in sync with the code, never written by hand.
Every API we ship is self-documented, fully type-safe, and structured so any engineer on the team can navigate it within minutes of cloning the repo.

posts.title: Why We Choose Next.js for Every Client Web Project
posts.excerpt: Server components, the App Router, and edge deployment have changed how we build. Here's our full reasoning.
posts.author: Enwiya Engineering

content.text: When a client asks us what framework to use for their web project, the answer is almost always Next.js. That recommendation has only gotten stronger since the App Router and React Server Components landed.
Server Components Change the Default

With React Server Components, data fetching happens on the server by default. Pages arrive pre-rendered with their data, load instantly, and send zero JavaScript for components that don't need interactivity. The performance baseline is dramatically better without any extra work.
The App Router Gives Us Real Layouts

Nested layouts, loading states, error boundaries, and streaming are all first-class in the App Router. We can build complex dashboard UIs with shared navigation that doesn't re-mount on every navigation — something that was painful to achieve before.

items: Automatic code-splitting per route
Built-in image optimisation with next/image
Edge runtime for sub-50ms API responses globally
First-class TypeScript support
Seamless Vercel or self-hosted deployment

content.text: Next.js lets us deliver production-grade performance without forcing clients into a complex infrastructure setup. That's the trade-off we want to make every time.

posts.title: How AI is Transforming Business Operations in 2026
posts.excerpt: From document processing to predictive analytics, we break down the practical AI use-cases we're deploying for clients right now.
posts.author: Enwiya Team

content.text: The AI hype cycle is behind us. What remains are real, deployable tools that can meaningfully reduce manual work, speed up decisions, and surface insights that were previously buried in data. Here is what we are actually building for clients in 2026.
Intelligent Document Processing

Contracts, invoices, intake forms — most businesses are still processing these by hand. We build pipelines that extract structured data from unstructured documents using LLMs combined with post-processing validation. A client recently cut their invoice processing time from 4 hours to 12 minutes per day.
Predictive Analytics Dashboards

Rather than dashboards that show what happened, we build dashboards that show what is likely to happen. Churn prediction, demand forecasting, and lead scoring models are now accessible to businesses without a data science team — the heavy lifting is done in the infrastructure we build.
Workflow Automation with AI Triggers

AI shines when it handles the decision layer of a workflow — routing support tickets to the right team, flagging anomalous transactions, or summarising long email threads before they reach a human. We wire these capabilities into existing tools via API.
The most valuable AI implementations are narrow, reliable, and solve one painful problem extremely well. We help clients find those opportunities and build them carefully.

posts.title: From MVP to Production: Our Development Process
posts.excerpt: A transparent look at how we take a product from a napkin sketch to a live, monitored, scalable production system.
posts.author: Enwiya Product

content.text: Most software projects fail not because of bad code but because of unclear requirements, scope creep, and a disconnect between what gets built and what users actually need. Our process is designed to prevent all three.
Phase 1: Discovery (Week 1–2)

Before a line of code is written, we run structured discovery sessions with key stakeholders. We map user journeys, define success metrics, and produce a written scope document that both sides sign off on. Ambiguity at this stage is expensive later.
Phase 2: Design & Architecture (Week 2–3)

We produce high-fidelity Figma prototypes and a system design document covering the data model, API contracts, and infrastructure choices. This acts as the single source of truth for engineers and surfaces design conflicts early.
Phase 3: Iterative Build (Week 3–N)

We work in two-week sprints with a demo at the end of each. Clients see working software every two weeks — not just status updates. Every sprint has a clearly defined goal and acceptance criteria agreed in advance.
Phase 4: Launch & Handover
We deploy to production with monitoring, alerting, and runbooks in place. Handover includes a recorded walkthrough, CI/CD documentation, and a 30-day support window.
Transparency is not optional at Enwiya. You see the code, the deploy logs, the metrics, and the plan — at every stage of the project.

posts.title: Cloud Cost Optimisation: Lessons from the Field
posts.excerpt: Five techniques we used to cut a client's AWS bill by 40% without sacrificing performance or reliability.
posts.author: Enwiya Engineering

content.text: A client came to us with a $22,000/month AWS bill and a suspicion they were over-provisioned. After a two-week audit and a phased migration, we brought that to $13,000/month — a 41% reduction — with zero downtime and no performance regressions.
What We Found

items: RDS instances sized for peak Black Friday load running 24/7 at 8% CPU average
Lambda functions with 3 GB memory allocations that needed 200 MB
S3 buckets with no lifecycle policies accumulating years of stale logs
NAT Gateway charges from services that could use VPC endpoints
On-demand EC2 for stable, predictable batch workloads

content.text: The Five Fixes

items: Right-sized RDS to the appropriate instance class with read replicas for reporting queries
Profiled and reduced Lambda memory across 60 functions using AWS Lambda Power Tuning
Added S3 Intelligent-Tiering and lifecycle rules to archive logs after 30 days
Replaced NAT Gateway traffic with VPC endpoints for S3 and DynamoDB
Moved batch jobs to Spot Instances with an on-demand fallback

content.text: Cloud cost is an engineering problem. The tools to fix it exist — the gap is usually just time and attention. An audit pays for itself in weeks.

posts.title: Case Study: Launching RetailCore in 8 Weeks
posts.excerpt: How we designed, built, and shipped a full-stack e-commerce platform for a retail client in under two months.
posts.author: Enwiya Team

content.text: RetailCore is a full-stack e-commerce platform we built for a regional retail chain that had outgrown their legacy system. The brief: replace an ageing Magento install with a modern, fast, maintainable platform — in eight weeks, before their peak season.
The Stack

items: Next.js 15 App Router for the storefront and admin dashboard
Node.js + TypeScript API with PostgreSQL via Prisma
Stripe for payments and subscription billing
Cloudflare for CDN and DDoS protection
GitHub Actions for CI/CD with staging and production environments

content.text: Week-by-Week Timeline

Weeks 1–2 were discovery and design. Weeks 3–5 were core build: product catalogue, cart, checkout, and order management. Weeks 6–7 were admin dashboard, reporting, and data migration from the legacy system. Week 8 was QA, load testing, and go-live.
Results at Launch

items: Page load time reduced from 4.8s to 0.9s (LCP)
Mobile conversion rate up 23% in the first month
Zero critical incidents in the first 60 days of operation
Admin team onboarded in a single half-day training session

content.text: Eight weeks is tight. It works when requirements are locked early, decisions are made fast, and the team has done it before. That's what we bring.

projects.title: Enwiya Law
projects.description: Enwiya Law Firm is a full-service legal practice dedicated to delivering strategic, client-first representation across a broad range of practice areas. From corporate transactions and estate planning to immigration and civil litigation, our attorneys bring deep expertise, careful judgment, and unwavering commitment to every case. We believe that access to quality legal counsel should be straightforward — so we take the time to understand your situation, explain your options clearly, and fight for the outcome you deserve.
